summary: Atwood observes the irony of people keeping physical calculators next to their computers and explores why this happens. He cites Jef Raskin's research showing that using a pocket calculator (7 seconds) is faster than launching the built-in calculator app (16 seconds). While Atwood demonstrates that keyboard shortcuts can beat the physical calculator, he acknowledges the process isn't simple. He argues this exposes a fundamental failure in GUI design—that computers still can't handle basic arithmetic as easily as a $5 calculator, and advocates for non-modal, OS-level calculation facilities.
key_points: Physical calculators persist alongside computers because launching calc.exe requires too many steps and context switches
Raskin's experiment showed pocket calculators were twice as fast as software calculators (7 vs 16 seconds)
Keyboard shortcuts can theoretically beat physical calculators, but the complexity defeats the purpose of simplicity
The real solution is OS-level, non-modal functionality that doesn't require spawning separate windows
Calculator mousepads are a symptom of GUI design failure, not user ignorance
money_quotes: Have you ever noticed how many people keep a physical calculator next to their computer? The irony is almost palpable.
It is much faster to grab a pocket calculator.
It took about 7 seconds... This took about 16 seconds.
I guarantee I could beat that 7 second time quoted for the physical calculator. But it's not exactly simple, is it?
Users with calculator mousepads are no laughing matter.
Whenever I see users like this, I'm reminded of how far we have to go in GUI design when the simple act of adding numbers together is so complicated.
key_insight: When users resort to dedicated hardware to perform tasks their computers should handle effortlessly, it reveals fundamental failures in software design rather than user incompetence.
